full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is not often that you walk into an Episcopal church and hear a sermon series.<|7.40|> <|8.02|> But if you were with us online or in person last week, and you're with us again today,<|13.78|> <|14.20|> you've entered a sermon series.<|16.72|> <|17.62|> Last week, Dean Richard highlighted a maxim about Episcopal biblical interpretation<|24.12|> <|24.12|> that I will highlight for you again today.<|27.68|> <|27.68|> Amen.<|28.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We take the Bible too seriously to take it literally.<|6.28|> <|7.22|> It might seem suspect that Richard and I have used this phrase on Sundays<|12.26|> <|12.26|> when the readings have mentioned hell, the gender binary, and divorce.<|18.64|> <|18.64|> Amen.<|19.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But I assure you that this is a principle of biblical interpretation<|6.00|> <|6.00|> Episcopalians attend to even when the text is not socially and politically charged.<|13.58|> <|14.68|> If you've been in one of St. John's education for ministry,<|18.52|> <|19.06|> catechumenate, or Episcopal 101 classes,<|21.74|> <|21.74|> you are already familiar with some of the basics of holistic biblical interpretation.<|28.86|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.90|> Dig for context when reading the Bible.<|4.30|> <|4.30|> Take note of who is writing, when they're writing,<|8.96|> <|8.96|> to whom they are writing, and why they are writing.<|13.50|> <|13.50|> In other words, context is paramount.<|17.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I'll say again what I've heard before.<|2.46|> <|3.46|> A text without context is a pretext for a subtext.<|8.40|> <|9.92|> We laugh because it's true.<|12.68|> <|13.74|> We laugh as a substitute for crying<|17.30|> <|17.30|> because our hearts are burdened by the stories of people<|22.08|> <|22.08|> who find themselves on the receiving end of painful things.<|27.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> biblical interpretation, what some might call theological malpractice.<|5.60|> <|7.04|> While textual context is very important,<|10.04|> <|11.32|> so is the context of the person or community interpreting sacred texts.<|18.56|> <|18.56|> Okay?<|19.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.90|> Yes, knowing that Mark's gospel is the earliest<|4.34|> <|4.34|> and shortest of the four is vital knowledge,<|8.50|> <|8.50|> but so is my self-awareness when interpreting.<|13.12|> <|14.50|> One of the great disservices of the fundamentalist movement<|17.32|> <|17.32|> of the early 20th century is the idea that Bible readers<|21.80|> <|21.80|> and interpreters are objective observers of the text.<|26.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You've heard people say it before.<|2.78|> <|3.18|> The Bible says it, I believe it, that settles it.<|6.32|> <|7.46|> It is impossible, and I feel comfortable<|10.22|> <|10.22|> using the word impossible here,<|11.98|> <|11.98|> to approach any text without the lens<|16.68|> <|16.68|> of one's cumulative experiences of domestic life,<|21.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> educational background, travel experiences, literary exposures,<|5.88|> <|5.88|> disability, race, gender, class, taste in movies, nationality,<|12.04|> <|12.04|> neighborhood, and a host of other factors.<|15.84|> <|15.84|> A host too innumerable to even name.<|20.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.30|> Imagine walking onto this holy ground,<|4.78|> <|4.78|> onto these sacred grounds at St. John's Cathedral today,<|8.52|> <|8.52|> fresh off the hills of a rocky divorce.<|12.66|> <|13.56|> Neither marriage counseling,<|15.68|> <|15.68|> the advice of trusted loved ones,<|17.62|> <|17.62|> without, nor a relocation to a brand new city<|21.54|> <|21.54|> without the intrusive presence of nosy neighbors<|25.28|> <|25.28|> could salvage a previously blissful marriage.<|29.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> Instead, you and your former spouse<|3.46|> <|3.46|> have decided to end things amicably<|6.30|> <|6.30|> and are determined to offer your children<|8.90|> <|8.90|> the best possible life with the two of you as their parents.<|12.92|> <|14.16|> You most likely are not coming here<|17.58|> <|17.58|> to witness a single unmarried preacher<|21.94|> <|21.94|> wax eloquent regarding divorce.<|25.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You most likely are here to receive the sacrament,<|7.00|> <|7.00|> enjoy a chat at coffee hour, and be on your way.<|13.00|> <|13.00|> That divorced person, friends in Christ,<|17.00|> <|17.00|> does not need one more load to carry.<|22.00|> <|22.00|> If anything, that divorced person<|25.00|> <|25.00|> just needs some space to breathe and pray.<|29.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> or you're an LGBTQ person who is confident in your personhood.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> You walk in after a long week at work and hear,<|12.00|> <|12.00|> "Therefore, a man leaves his father and his mother<|16.00|> <|16.00|> and clings to his wife, and they become one flesh."<|21.00|> <|21.00|> And you think to yourself, "I don't need this lecture again."<|27.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You usually have the energy to fight and debate the biblical text used to negate and erase your<|5.74|> <|5.74|> embodied experiences, but today you just don't have it in you. You just want another uneventful,<|12.92|> <|13.38|> unremarkable Sunday in church, and you deserve an uneventful, unremarkable Sunday in church.<|19.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You want for the life of you, for Christians and other people, to stop being so simplistic<|7.24|> <|7.24|> when it comes to sacred texts.<|10.36|> <|10.36|> You echo the sentiment of Hebrew Bible scholar and Episcopal priest, Dr. Will Gaffney, who<|16.38|> <|16.38|> says, "What I think people miss when reading the stories of the scripture is the text is<|23.12|> <|23.12|> a lot like an iceberg."<|25.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And we all know that there's more of the iceberg<|3.44|> <|3.44|> underwater than on top of the water.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> But we don't know what the terrain is like.<|10.44|> <|10.44|> So you don't know if under the water,<|12.64|> <|12.64|> the iceberg is doing this or doing that.<|15.84|> <|15.84|> So reading the text in English without resorting<|18.46|> <|18.46|> to the original languages means you just don't know<|21.46|> <|21.46|> what you're omitting underneath."<|25.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Dr. Gaffney's wisdom regarding textual interpretation is applicable to the ways in which we all<|7.70|> <|7.70|> interpret the text not only of the Bible, but of our lives every day.<|14.36|> <|14.36|> And when we encounter texts held sacred to our shared Christian faith.<|20.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> said another way.<|1.76|> <|2.66|> When I see someone walking through these doors<|5.60|> <|5.60|> or in line at King Soopers<|8.26|> <|8.26|> or cutting me off on I-25,<|10.90|> <|11.66|> I am just seeing the part of the iceberg I can see,<|16.88|> <|17.42|> that I can perceive.<|19.78|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> there is more to that person than I can even begin to imagine.<|5.32|> <|6.64|> When I am tempted to oversimplify someone's social context or life experience,<|12.26|> <|12.26|> may I hear God say, they are not that simple.<|17.80|> <|18.86|> It's not that simple.<|20.98|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This is an exercise in generosity, a way of saying,<|6.00|> <|6.00|> "When I'm reading the Bible individually or in community with other people,<|11.00|> <|11.00|> I will extend as much grace as possible to those who might find themselves<|17.00|> <|17.00|> on the underside of my interpretation."<|21.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I will not interpret based on the strongest and most prominent person in the room.<|5.62|> <|6.62|> Instead, I'll let the most vulnerable and least powerful person in the room<|12.62|> <|12.62|> be the true north of my interpretive work.<|16.76|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is in a generous, spacious, gracious interpretation of the text that I can hear<|8.22|> <|8.22|> Jesus' words in Matthew urgently and with great sharpness. My yoke is easy and my burden is light.<|18.78|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This leads us then, as we close,<|4.64|> <|4.64|> to a series of probing questions that come in handy<|9.38|> <|9.38|> every time we find ourselves in the bumpy terrain<|12.82|> <|12.82|> of biblical interpretation.<|15.38|> <|15.38|> First, does my interpretation of a given text or situation<|23.38|> <|23.38|> make someone's life easier?<|27.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Does it make their circumstances more bearable?<|3.60|> <|4.48|> Does it lighten their load?<|6.50|> <|7.96|> Does it bring an end to their oppression?<|11.28|> <|12.82|> Does it make God's love for them louder and more obvious?<|16.64|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Additionally, when I read the scriptures, am I just picking the low-hanging fruit of simplistic interpretation,<|8.78|> <|8.78|> or am I giving myself the space to be more empathetic and curious as an interpreter of the text?<|16.98|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> - Yes.<|1.22|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.76|> Second,<|3.04|> <|4.76|> who is and isn't getting the benefit of the doubt<|9.76|> <|9.76|> when I make pronouncements about whole categories of people?<|13.44|> <|14.72|> Who could potentially be marginalized or stigmatized<|19.08|> <|19.08|> as a result of my interpretation?<|22.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And finally, and this is a prayer, and I always recommend just when you're reading the Bible,<|7.24|> <|7.46|> pray. Pray when you're reading. God, will you show me what, and more importantly, who I'm missing here?<|19.12|> <|20.26|> Who is missing from the text? Who is missing from this room?<|25.90|> <|25.90|> Who is missing from this room?<|27.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As you tackle these questions and allow them to tackle you,<|5.50|> <|6.38|> I pray that Jesus Christ, God's word incarnate,<|10.94|> <|11.24|> will make each of us more contextual, more compassionate,<|15.98|> <|16.56|> and more curious toward those with whom we are making this journey.<|21.08|> <|22.18|> Amen.<|22.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|3.20|><|endoftext|>
